subject: Psychology
grade_subject_fit: Source-derived Cycle A cognition package aligned to the master spec: evidence tasks, tiered work, decision plus reason, and safe classroom examples.
unit_context: Cycle A A5 examines attention, memory, problem solving, intelligence, and limits of measurement.
assignment_purpose: Students use evidence to explain cognitive performance without treating one score or one mistake as a complete label.
final_evidence_target: A cognitive case analysis with claim, evidence, strategy choice, reason, and limit.

student_task_flow.label: Cognition concepts
student_task_flow.evidence: Students define attention, encoding, storage, retrieval, working memory, long-term memory, bias, intelligence, validity, reliability.

student_task_flow.label: Fictional learning scenarios
student_task_flow.evidence: Students connect memory strategies and thinking errors to evidence.

student_task_flow.label: Strategy recommendation
student_task_flow.evidence: Students recommend a strategy and explain why it fits the evidence.

success_criteria: I can explain memory as a process.
I can distinguish attention, encoding, storage, and retrieval.
I can evaluate a cognitive strategy from evidence.
I can make a decision and give a reason.
I can avoid overclaiming from one score or task.
differentiation_model: Supported students label parts of the memory process. Proficient students choose the best strategy for a case. Extending students evaluate measurement limits and alternative explanations.
checkpoint_release_logic: Students complete vocabulary and process map before final strategy recommendation.
teacher_implementation_notes: Avoid ranking students by intelligence or memory ability.
Treat cognitive performance as task, context, strategy, and evidence rather than identity.
Require limits when interpreting scores or task performance.
likely_misconceptions: Memory works like a video recording.
Forgetting always means information is gone.
A strategy works equally for every task.
pacing_shape: Five-part package: vocabulary, memory process, thinking strategies, measurement limits, final recommendation.

teacher_guide.title: Cycle A A5 Teacher Guide — Thinking, Memory, and Intelligence
teacher_guide.overview: Students learn how attention, memory, problem solving, and measurement shape cognitive performance. The teacher keeps the lesson away from ranking or labelling people.
teacher_guide.essential_question: How can we explain thinking and memory performance without reducing people to one score or one mistake?
teacher_guide.big_idea: Cognitive performance depends on process, strategy, task demands, context, and measurement limits.
teacher_guide.opening_frame: A careful cognition explanation asks what the task required, what process may be involved, what strategy was used, and what the evidence cannot prove.
teacher_guide.frame_line: A5 frame: task, process, strategy, evidence, limit.
teacher_guide.assessment_focus: Final work should identify a cognitive process, recommend a strategy, justify it with evidence, and include a measurement or context limit.
learning_goals: Define attention, encoding, storage, retrieval, and working memory.
Evaluate limits of scores and measures.
Make a decision and justify it.
integrity_checks: No ranking students by cognitive ability.
No personal disclosure of test scores.
teacher_notes: When students say someone is smart or not smart, redirect to task demands, strategy, practice, and evidence.
When students treat memory as a recording, use encoding and retrieval examples.
For extension, students critique what a measure can and cannot show.
questions: What does the measure not show?

timing.activity: Map attention, encoding, storage, and retrieval.

model.title: Strategy recommendation model
steps: Claim: retrieval practice may help the character remember more effectively.
Evidence: the case shows rereading without checking memory.
Strategy: use practice questions and spacing.
Alternative: sleep, task difficulty, and distraction may also matter.
Limit: one quiz result does not define ability.

content.scenario: A fictional student rereads notes but does not test recall.

content.right: Stronger: a score shows performance on one task under certain conditions.

rows: Do not reduce people to performance labels.

task_sheet.title: A5 Student Packet — Thinking, Memory, and Intelligence
task_sheet.purpose_line: Use fictional cases to explain cognition with process, strategy, evidence, and limits.
instructions: Make a decision and give a reason.

tasks.label: Vocabulary organizer
tasks.prompt: Define attention, encoding, storage, retrieval, working memory, long-term memory, bias, intelligence, validity, reliability.

tasks.label: Supported pathway
tasks.prompt: Label the memory process in a fictional study scenario.

tasks.label: Proficient pathway
tasks.prompt: Choose the best strategy for the case and explain your evidence.

tasks.label: Extending pathway
tasks.prompt: Evaluate what a quiz score can and cannot show about cognitive performance.

tasks.label: Final assessment
tasks.prompt: Write a cognitive strategy recommendation using process, evidence, decision plus reason, and limit.

exit_ticket.title: A5 Exit Ticket
exit_ticket.prompt: What cognitive strategy would you recommend for the fictional case, and what is one limit?
